does: This paper introduces Contextual Decomposition for Transformers (CD-T), a mathematical framework for identifying interpretable circuits in large language models by isolating the contribution of individual model features through recursive computation on computational graphs. Unlike existing activation patching methods, CD-T efficiently produces circuits at multiple levels of abstraction, from attention heads at specific sequence positions to higher-level components, without requiring non-zero gradients or domain-specific metrics. By combining contribution calculation with pruning, the method reduces circuit discovery runtime from hours to seconds while maintaining or improving accuracy in identifying task-relevant subgraphs within transformer models.
connects: This work extends the mechanistic interpretability research program that seeks to understand neural network internals through automated circuit discovery, building on prior activation patching approaches that identify task-specific subgraphs. CD-T addresses documented limitations of existing methods—slow runtime, approximation errors, metric-specific requirements—by applying contextual decomposition, a technique from feature attribution that has shown promise in other domains but has not been efficiently scaled to transformer-level circuit discovery. The paper responds to the broader challenge of scaling explanations beyond toy models, connecting to concurrent efforts in automated interpretability that aim to make circuit discovery tractable for large language models.
enables: This efficient framework opens pathways for scaling mechanistic interpretation to larger and more complex models by making circuit discovery practical rather than prohibitively expensive. The ability to discover fine-grained circuits at sequence position and attention head resolution enables deeper investigation of how transformers implement specific computations, potentially facilitating the discovery of bugs, biases, or unintended behaviors. CD-T's multi-scale decomposition approach could support the development of pruning techniques, model compression methods, and targeted interventions on specific learned features, while also providing foundations for understanding emergent behaviors and improving model transparency in safety-critical applications.
